title: While One Farm Owner Turned Down $26M From an AI Data Center Developer, Another Couple Sold Their Struggling Farm for $22M
excerpt: As artificial intelligence fuels a massive data center construction boom across America, some landowners are cashing in on life-changing offers while others...
content_html:
Benzinga and Yahoo Finance LLC may earn commission or revenue on some items through the links below.

As artificial intelligence fuels a massive data center construction boom across America, some landowners are cashing in on life-changing offers while others are refusing to sell at any price.

That contrast was on full display in Kentucky and Pennsylvania. In one case, a farming family reportedly turned down a $26 million offer to preserve land that has been in their family for generations. In the other, a struggling farm became the source of a $22 million windfall that transformed a couple's financial future.

Don't Miss:

  • Deloitte's #1 Fastest-Growing Software Company Lets Users Earn Money Just by Scrolling — Investors Can Still Get In at $0.52/Share

  • If there was a new fund backed by Jeff Bezos offering a 7-9% target yield with monthly dividends would you invest in it?

Two Families, Two Very Different Decisions

Ida Huddleston and her family rejected a $26 million offer for part of their 1,200-acre farm outside Maysville, Kentucky, according to WKRC-TV. The land was being targeted for a proposed AI data center by an unidentified company.

Huddleston's daughter, Delsia Bare, said the family never seriously considered selling.

"I'll stay and hold and feed a nation," Bare told WKRC-TV. "Twenty-six million [dollars] doesn't mean anything."

The family said their roots on the property stretch back generations. Bare added that relatives farmed the land through the Great Depression and helped produce food during difficult times in American history.

Huddleston was skeptical about promises that a data center would bring jobs and economic growth to the area.

"They call us old stupid farmers, you know, but we're not," she told WKRC-TV. "We know whenever our food is disappearing, our lands are disappearing, and we don't have any water."

Trending: There's More Than One Way To Put Cash To Work. Some Accredited Investors Are Looking Beyond Savings Accounts.

Despite the family's refusal, developers reportedly revised their plans using neighboring properties whose owners agreed to sell, meaning the project could still move forward nearby.

A few hundred miles away in Pennsylvania, another farming family faced a similar offer and came to a very different conclusion.

David and Marilee Kiliti initially thought developers were joking when they suggested their 89-acre farm could be worth more than $20 million, according to The Wall Street Journal.

The couple had spent years struggling to make a living from the property. David took construction jobs while Marilee worked as a forklift driver before retiring.

content_html_zh:
Benzinga 和 Yahoo Finance LLC 可能通过以下链接赚取某些项目的佣金或收入。

随着人工智能推动美国各地大规模的数据中心建设热潮，一些土地所有者正在从改变生活的报价中获利，而另一些土地所有者则拒绝以任何价格出售。

这种对比在肯塔基州和宾夕法尼亚州得到了充分体现。 据报道，在一个案例中，一个农业家庭拒绝了价值 2600 万美元的报价，以保护其家族世代拥有的土地。 另一方面，一个苦苦挣扎的农场却带来了 2200 万美元的意外之财，改变了一对夫妇的财务未来。

不要错过：

  • 德勤排名第一的增长最快的软件公司让用户只需滚动即可赚钱——投资者仍然可以以 0.52 美元/股的价格入股
  • 如果杰夫·贝索斯 (Jeff Bezos) 支持的新基金提供 7-9% 的目标收益率和每月股息，您会投资它吗？

两个家庭，两个截然不同的决定

据 WKRC-TV 报道，艾达·赫德尔斯顿 (Ida Huddleston) 和她的家人拒绝了 2600 万美元收购肯塔基州梅斯维尔郊外 1,200 英亩农场部分土地的报价。 一家身份不明的公司计划将这块土地用于建设人工智能数据中心。

赫德尔斯顿的女儿德尔西娅·巴尔 (Delsia Bare) 表示，家人从未认真考虑过出售。

“我会留下来，支撑并养活一个国家，”贝尔告诉 WKRC-TV。 “两千六百万[美元]没有任何意义。”

这家人说，他们在这片土地上的根可以追溯到几代人之前。 巴尔补充说，亲戚们在大萧条时期耕种土地，并在美国历史上的困难时期帮助生产粮食。

赫德尔斯顿对数据中心将为该地区带来就业和经济增长的承诺持怀疑态度。

她对 WKRC-TV 表示：“他们称我们为愚蠢的老农民，你知道，但我们不是。” “我们知道，每当我们的食物消失时，我们的土地就会消失，我们也没有水。”

趋势：让现金发挥作用的方法不止一种。 一些合格的投资者正在关注储蓄账户以外的领域。

据报道，尽管这家人拒绝，但开发商利用业主同意出售的邻近房产修改了计划，这意味着该项目仍可以在附近推进。

在几百英里外的宾夕法尼亚州，另一个农民家庭也面临着类似的提议，但得出了截然不同的结论。

据《华尔街日报》报道，当开发商暗示他们的 89 英亩农场价值可能超过 2000 万美元时，David 和 Marilee Kiliti 最初以为开发商是在开玩笑。

这对夫妇多年来一直靠房产谋生。 大卫在退休前从事建筑工作，而玛丽莉则担任叉车司机。
